decision_source: Whether to expose your Anthropic API key directly in client-side code or route all API calls through a server-side proxy
stem: A startup is launching a customer-facing web chat application powered by Claude. To ship quickly, the frontend engineer proposes embedding the Anthropic API key directly in the JavaScript bundle so the browser can call the Anthropic API without a backend. The security lead pushes back and insists all API calls must be routed through a server-side proxy instead. What is the primary reason the server-side proxy approach is required for production deployments?

options.A: A server-side proxy allows the team to rotate the API key without redeploying the frontend, which is the main operational advantage over client-side storage.
options.B: Any user can inspect the JavaScript bundle or intercept network requests to extract the embedded key, allowing them to make API calls billed to the owner with no access controls.
options.C: Environment variables injected at build time are compiled into the JavaScript bundle the same way a hardcoded string is, so neither approach exposes the key to end users.
options.D: Routing requests through a proxy adds a network hop that increases latency, but it is required because the Anthropic API enforces same-origin restrictions that block direct browser requests.

decision_source: Whether to specify explicit format, length, and audience constraints in a system prompt or rely on Claude to infer appropriate defaults from a brief task description
stem: A SaaS company deploys Claude as a customer support assistant with the system prompt: 'You are a helpful customer support agent.' Within two weeks, the support team reports that responses range from a single sentence to six-paragraph essays depending on query complexity, breaking the UI card layout and generating customer complaints about verbosity. The engineering lead proposes adding a retry loop that detects responses exceeding 150 words and re-prompts Claude to shorten them. What is the most architecturally sound response to this problem?

options.A: Prepend a formatting instruction to every user message at the application layer — for example, 'Reply in 2-3 sentences.' — so that length constraints are enforced per request without modifying the shared system prompt.
options.B: Add explicit constraints to the system prompt specifying maximum response length, tone, reading level, and output structure so Claude has no ambiguity about expected format.
options.C: Implement the retry loop as proposed — Claude will converge on the correct length after one reformatting pass without requiring prompt changes.
options.D: Fine-tune a model on a curated set of approved support responses so that consistent length and tone become an inherent behavioral property rather than a runtime instruction.

decision_source: Whether to specify explicit format, length, and audience constraints in a system prompt or rely on Claude to infer appropriate defaults from a brief task description
stem: In the lab, you initialized the Anthropic client and passed a system prompt to the `messages.create()` call. You are now testing a customer support assistant. When you pass `system='You are a helpful customer support agent.'` and send five different user queries ranging from simple to complex, you observe that the `content[0].text` responses vary significantly in length and occasionally include markdown formatting. Which change to your `messages.create()` call most directly resolves the inconsistency at the source?

options.A: Add `temperature=0` to the `messages.create()` call to make outputs deterministic and eliminate length variance.
options.B: Update the `system` parameter to explicitly state the maximum number of sentences, prohibit markdown, specify plain prose, and define the target reading level for all responses.
options.C: Wrap the `messages.create()` call in a loop that checks `len(content[0].text)` and retries with an appended instruction if the response exceeds a threshold.
options.D: Set `max_tokens=150` to establish a consistent response ceiling across all query types.

decision_source: Whether to design the five-step request lifecycle with synchronous blocking calls or implement streaming to progressively deliver Claude's response to end users
stem: A consumer-facing writing assistant generates responses between 500 and 1500 tokens. During beta testing, the team observes that 34% of users close the tab before seeing any output, and session recordings confirm most abandonment occurs between the 8 and 12 second mark — before the full response has loaded. The product manager proposes adding a spinner animation and an optimistic placeholder ('Your content is being generated...') to signal progress. What is the most architecturally sound response to the abandonment problem?

options.A: Implement response streaming so the first tokens reach the client within one second, reducing perceived wait time regardless of total response length.
options.B: Add the spinner and placeholder UI as proposed — users who see activity indicators are significantly less likely to abandon than users who see a blank screen.
options.C: Reduce max_tokens to cap responses at 300 tokens, which brings synchronous delivery time under the 5-second threshold users tolerate.
options.D: Establish a persistent WebSocket connection to the server but continue buffering the full response before forwarding it to the client, eliminating HTTP overhead without requiring streaming changes in the application layer.
